title: [InstSimplify] Discard unnecessary `select` when the conditions are `ICmps` with EQ
author: spaits
description:
This PR was motivated by the discussion with @dtcxzyw in PR #177410. That PR has caused a regression: https://github.com/dtcxzyw/llvm-opt-benchmark/pull/3371#discussion_r2722785220 .

This PR tries to address that. It addresses the issue before the optimization added in #177410 runs. When I looked at the pre regression state of the code I noticed that one of the select isn't needed:
```llvm
define i32 @src(i32 %6, i32 %44) {
  %46 = icmp eq i32 %44, 4
  %47 = icmp eq i32 %6, 4
    
  %..i = select i1 %47, i32 %44, i32 %6
  %.0.i8 = select i1 %46, i32 %6, i32 %..i
  ; Return the %6, if that isn't 4 otherwise return %47
  ret i32 %.0.i8
}
    
define i32 @tgt(i32 %6, i32 %44) {
  %47 = icmp eq i32 %6, 4
  %selR = select i1 %47, i32 %44, i32 %6
  ret i32 %selR
}
```
So it is good enogh if we just gave the first select. In that case #177410 won't be run and we have even better code than before.

Alive2: https://alive2.llvm.org/ce/z/GVtLBT .

tests.test_body:
define i32 @selectICmpSelectInArmNotSimplifiedPoisonFlagOnCondUseDefChain(i32 %0, i32 %1, i1 %scond) {
  %3 = icmp eq i32 %0, %1
  %withPoisonFlag = or disjoint i32 %0, 42
  %sameValues = icmp ule i32 %withPoisonFlag, %1
  %4 = select i1 %sameValues, i32 %1, i32 %0
  %5 = select i1 %3, i32 %0, i32 %4
  ret i32 %5
}

define i32 @selectICmpSelectInArmNotSimplifiedSecondSelectPoisonBarrier(i32 %0, i32 %1, i1 %scond) {
  %3 = icmp eq i32 %0, %1
  %sameValues = icmp ule i32 %0, %1
  %or = or i1 %sameValues, %scond
  %4 = select i1 %or, i32 %1, i32 %0
  %5 = select i1 %3, i32 %0, i32 %4
  ret i32 %5
}

define i32 @selectICmpSelectInArmSimplifiedDueToSameValues(i32 %0, i32 %1, i1 %scond) {
  %3 = icmp eq i32 %0, %1
  %sameValues = icmp ule i32 %0, %1
  %4 = select i1 %sameValues, i32 %1, i32 %0
  %5 = select i1 %3, i32 %0, i32 %4
  ret i32 %5
}

define i32 @selectICmpSelectInArmSimplifiedNoPoisonFlagOnCondUseDefChain(i32 %0, i32 %1, i1 %scond) {
  %3 = icmp eq i32 %0, %1
  %withPoisonFlag = or i32 %0, 42
  %sameValues = icmp ule i32 %withPoisonFlag, %1
  %4 = select i1 %sameValues, i32 %1, i32 %0
  %5 = select i1 %3, i32 %0, i32 %4
  ret i32 %5
}

define i32 @selectICmpSelectInArmNotSimplifiedSecondSelectPoisonBarrierPoisonFlagInCond(i32 %0, i32 %1, i1 %scond) {
  %3 = icmp eq i32 %0, %1
  %sameValues = icmp ule i32 %0, %1
  %or = or disjoint i1 %sameValues, %scond
  %4 = select i1 %or, i32 %1, i32 %0
  %5 = select i1 %3, i32 %0, i32 %4
  ret i32 %5
}

define i32 @AndICmpAnd2(i32 %0, i32 %1) {
  %3 = icmp eq i32 %1, 4
  %4 = icmp eq i32 %0, 4
  %or.cond.i = and i1 %4, %3
  %5 = and i1 %4, true
  %6 = or i1 %3, %5
  %spec.select.i = select i1 %6, i32 %0, i32 %1
  %.0.i8 = select i1 %or.cond.i, i32 4, i32 %spec.select.i
  ret i32 %.0.i8
}

define i32 @negative_CmpUsed(i32 %0, i32 %1) {
  %3 = icmp eq i32 %1, 4
  %4 = icmp eq i32 %0, 4
  %or.cond.i = and i1 %4, %3
  %5 = xor i1 %4, true
  %6 = or i1 %3, %5
  %spec.select.i = select i1 %6, i32 %0, i32 %1
  %.0.i8 = select i1 %or.cond.i, i32 4, i32 %spec.select.i
  call void @use32(i1 %or.cond.i)
  ret i32 %.0.i8
}

declare void @use32(i1)

define i32 @AndICmp(i32 %0, i32 %1, i32 %val) {
  %3 = icmp eq i32 %1, %val
  %4 = icmp eq i32 %0, %val
  %or.cond.i = and i1 %4, %3
  %5 = xor i1 %4, true
  %6 = or i1 %3, %5
  %spec.select.i = select i1 %6, i32 %0, i32 %1
  %.0.i8 = select i1 %or.cond.i, i32 %val, i32 %spec.select.i
  ret i32 %.0.i8
}

define i32 @AndICmpConstant(i32 %0, i32 %1) {
  %3 = icmp eq i32 %1, 4
  %4 = icmp eq i32 %0, 4
  %or.cond.i = and i1 %4, %3
  %5 = xor i1 %4, true
  %6 = or i1 %3, %5
  %spec.select.i = select i1 %6, i32 %0, i32 %1
  %.0.i8 = select i1 %or.cond.i, i32 4, i32 %spec.select.i
  ret i32 %.0.i8
}

define i32 @negative_firstSelectPoisonBarrier(i32 %0, i32 %1, i1 %cond) {
  %3 = icmp eq i32 %1, 4
  %4 = icmp eq i32 %0, 4
  %or.cond.i = and i1 %4, %3
  %spec.select.i = select i1 %cond, i32 %0, i32 %1
  %.0.i8 = select i1 %or.cond.i, i32 4, i32 %spec.select.i
  ret i32 %.0.i8
}

define i32 @negative_condMorePoisonThanArm(i32 %0, i32 %1) {
  %3 = icmp eq i32 %1, 4
  %4 = icmp eq i32 %0, 4
  %or.cond.i = and i1 %4, %3
  %5 = and i1 %4, true
  %6 = or disjoint i1 %3, %5
  %spec.select.i = select i1 %6, i32 %0, i32 %1
  %.0.i8 = select i1 %or.cond.i, i32 4, i32 %spec.select.i
  ret i32 %.0.i8
}

define i32 @AndICmpRemovedNonConst(i32 %0, i32 %1, i32 %val) {
  %3 = icmp eq i32 %1, %val
  %4 = icmp eq i32 %0, %val
  %or.cond.i = and i1 %4, %3
  %5 = xor i1 %4, true
  %spec.select.i = select i1 %5, i32 %0, i32 %1
  %.0.i8 = select i1 %or.cond.i, i32 %val, i32 %spec.select.i
  ret i32 %.0.i8
}

define i32 @negative_selectCondOnlyMutualOneArm(i32 %0, i32 %1) {
  %3 = icmp eq i32 %1, 4
  %4 = icmp eq i32 %0, 4
  %or.cond.i = and i1 %4, %3
  %5 = xor i1 %4, true
  %spec.select.i = select i1 %5, i32 %0, i32 %1
  %.0.i8 = select i1 %or.cond.i, i32 4, i32 %spec.select.i
  ret i32 %.0.i8
}

define i32 @AndICmpAnd(i32 %0, i32 %1) {
  %3 = icmp eq i32 %1, 4
  %4 = icmp eq i32 %0, 4
  %or.cond.i = and i1 %4, %3
  %5 = xor i1 %4, true
  %6 = and i1 %3, %5
  %spec.select.i = select i1 %6, i32 %0, i32 %1
  %.0.i8 = select i1 %or.cond.i, i32 4, i32 %spec.select.i
  ret i32 %.0.i8
}

comments.author: spaits
comments.body:
So basically I should just try match to the cannonical form (select in select and the binops) in `simplifySelectInst` and call `simplifySelectWithEquivalence`. I could try that.

I tried first with this because the pattern before #177410 seemed pretty generic to me and I thought addressing the more generic case could bring more benefit since that is easier to catch and optimize.

comments.author: spaits
comments.body:
I have experimented with `simplifySelectWithEquivalence`. It works, if I add some code to the branch that runs when refinement is not allowed: https://github.com/spaits/llvm-project/commit/2bb7ec68b54a29e8583cb8487d4df1345c4543b5 . I am not yet 100% sure that what I added there is correct.

I still have testing to do, but I hope that commit shows a good direction.

comments.body:
Miscompilation reproducer:
```
define i32 @foo6(i32 %0, i32 %1) {
  %3 = icmp eq i32 %1, 4
  %4 = icmp eq i32 %0, 4
  %or.cond.i = and i1 %4, %3
  %5 = and i1 %4, true
  %6 = or disjoint i1 %3, %5
  %spec.select.i = select i1 %6, i32 %0, i32 %1
  %.0.i8 = select i1 %or.cond.i, i32 4, i32 %spec.select.i
  ret i32 %.0.i8
}
```
```
----------------------------------------
define i32 @foo6(i32 %#0, i32 %#1) {
#2:
  %#3 = icmp eq i32 %#1, 4
  %#4 = icmp eq i32 %#0, 4
  %or.cond.i = and i1 %#4, %#3
  %#5 = and i1 %#4, 1
  %#6 = or disjoint i1 %#3, %#5
  %spec.select.i = select i1 %#6, i32 %#0, i32 %#1
  %.0.i8 = select i1 %or.cond.i, i32 4, i32 %spec.select.i
  ret i32 %.0.i8
}
=>
define i32 @foo6(i32 %#0, i32 %#1) {
#2:
  %#3 = icmp eq i32 %#1, 4
  %#4 = icmp eq i32 %#0, 4
  %#5 = or disjoint i1 %#3, %#4
  %spec.select.i = select i1 %#5, i32 %#0, i32 %#1
  ret i32 %spec.select.i
}
Transformation doesn't verify!

ERROR: Target is more poisonous than source

NOTE: The counterexample is unique.

Example:
i32 %#0 = #x00000004 (4)
i32 %#1 = #x00000004 (4)

Source:
i1 %#3 = #x1 (1)
i1 %#4 = #x1 (1)
i1 %or.cond.i = #x1 (1)
i1 %#5 = #x1 (1)
i1 %#6 = poison
i32 %spec.select.i = poison
i32 %.0.i8 = #x00000004 (4)

Target:
i1 %#3 = #x1 (1)
i1 %#4 = #x1 (1)
i1 %#5 = poison
i32 %spec.select.i = poison
Source value: #x00000004 (4)
Target value: poison
```

comments.body:
I have addressed some of the reviews, but not everything yet. I still want to spilt the PR, add more tests, clean up tests, handle the `or` case. I am also working on those.

But, for now I would like to ask you to check if my poison checks are correct? I added some more tests to showcase the current state of the PR.

One of the interesting cases was when the first selects condition depended on different values than the second ones. In that case there were scenarios when the second select's poison barrier proprty is needed, therfore it must be kept. I could only address those cases in `simplifySelectInst`. I hope that's fine.

Thank you for your patience and assistance.

comments.author: spaits
comments.body:
I finally had some time and I have tried to continue with the issue but I couldn't really make any meaningful progress. I would like to share some of my observations and ask a few questions.

So I tried to split the PR into two. And tried to write test cases for the first PR. (Just the select simplify wo refinment.)
I had the following goal:
- I wanted this to work, since it's correct: https://alive2.llvm.org/ce/z/GditAm
- I wanted to avoid this incorrect transform from happening: https://alive2.llvm.org/ce/z/hBn55q

So the difference between the two `src`s is how the conditions of the `select`s relate to one other. From a poison point of view. In the working case the two condition are poisons in the exact same case. They are made up of the same values. While in the second case the first select is more poisonus, since it is made up from one more value.

For this reason I don't think what @nikic has suggested, that checking if the new condition won't introduce any new poison would work. 

Maybe each time a simplification happens and a select is thrown away, we shuld check if the select was acting as a poiosn barrier for a previous select? But this doesn't really sound right to me.

Maybe I should start thinking about moving things to InstCombine? @dtcxzyw you have made that suggestion but in a different context before.

I am not exactly sure of this can be addressed in `simplifyWithOpsReplaced`. I have an intuition, that maybe thinking of checking if the condition is made up from exactly the select arm, then the optimization would be good to go. But I am not sure...

I would really appriciat your inputs.

comments.author: spaits
comments.body:
I have updated the PR to build on top of #183291 (The currently draft PR with the non refining select ops). Sadly that PR in itself didn't bring any improvemnet.

I have restructured the tesing (Alive2 proofs: https://alive2.llvm.org/ce/z/G-w2Xf).
I know that there are more tests to be written, but first I would like to ask @dtcxzyw and @nikic whether this is the good direction to solve the issue.

This patch, and the predecessor patch, that introduces a recursive function with the recursion limit of 2, may be too expensive, when considering what we can gain from it. Also this would raise the recursion limit to 3 in `directlyImpliesPoison` (needed for the motivating case to work).

Are these costs too much?

Thank you for checking it out in advance!
